era_context: The early 16th century was a period of European Renaissance, religious ferment, and the beginning of global maritime expansion. The Spanish Empire was consolidating power under Ferdinand and Isabella's successors; the Portuguese Empire was expanding in the Indian Ocean; and European explorers were beginning to venture into the Atlantic and beyond. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty ruled China; the Ottoman Empire was consolidating power under Selim I; and the Aztec Empire in Mesoamerica was at its height.

events.title: Martin Luther Posts 95 Theses; Protestant Reformation Begins
events.description: On 31 October 1517 (All Saints' Eve), Martin Luther, an Augustinian friar and theology professor at the University of Wittenberg, posted 95 theses (propositions for debate) on the church door in Wittenberg, challenging the Catholic Church's practice of selling indulgences (remissions of sin in exchange for money). Luther's theses were written in Latin and were intended as an invitation to academic debate, but they quickly spread throughout the Holy Roman Empire and beyond, translated into German and other languages. The theses attacked papal authority, the concept of purgatory, and the Church's financial practices. While Luther did not initially intend to break with the Church, his challenge to papal authority and his emphasis on Scripture as the sole source of Christian doctrine became the foundation of the Protestant Reformation. This event is traditionally marked as the beginning of the Reformation, though the process was gradual and complex.
key_figures: Frederick III, Elector of Saxony (Luther's protector)
Johann Tetzel (Dominican friar, seller of indulgences)

sources.name: Luther's 95 Theses (Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum)

events.certainty_note: The date of 31 October 1517 for Luther's posting of the 95 Theses is universally accepted, though some scholars note that the exact method of posting (whether literally nailed to the church door or distributed in other ways) is debated. The theses themselves are well-documented and widely available.

events.title: Ottoman Empire Consolidates Control of Egypt and the Levant; Selim I Defeats Mamluk Sultanate
events.description: In 1517, the Ottoman Empire, under Sultan Selim I, defeated the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt in a series of military campaigns (the Mamluk-Ottoman War, 1516–1517). The Ottomans captured Cairo on 20 January 1517 and subsequently conquered the Levant (Syria, Palestine, and parts of Iraq). This conquest gave the Ottoman Empire control over the holy cities of Mecca and Medina, as well as the Suez Canal and the Red Sea trade routes. The conquest of Egypt and the Levant made the Ottoman Empire the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East, and established Ottoman control over the Islamic holy sites, which enhanced the sultan's prestige and authority. The Mamluk Sultanate, which had ruled Egypt for centuries, was effectively ended.
key_figures: Tuman Bay II (Mamluk Sultan, defeated and executed)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Takvim-i Vekayi and later Ottoman histories)

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman conquest of Egypt and the Levant in 1516–1517 is well-documented in Ottoman, Mamluk, and Venetian sources. The capture of Cairo on 20 January 1517 is universally accepted.

events.title: Portuguese Establish Trading Post in Colombo, Ceylon (Sri Lanka)
events.description: In 1517, Portuguese explorers, under the direction of the Portuguese crown, established a trading post in Colombo, Ceylon (present-day Sri Lanka). This was part of the broader Portuguese expansion in the Indian Ocean under the leadership of Afonso de Albuquerque. The Portuguese trading post in Colombo gave the Portuguese access to cinnamon and other valuable spices, and established Portuguese naval presence in the Indian Ocean. This expansion was part of the Portuguese strategy to control the spice trade and to establish a network of trading posts and fortresses throughout the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese viceroy in India)
events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Portuguese trading post in Colombo in 1517 is documented in Portuguese colonial records, though the exact date may vary slightly. The event is accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Aztec Empire at Its Height; Montezuma II Rules Tenochtitlan
events.description: In 1517, the Aztec Empire, under Emperor Montezuma II (who had ruled since 1502), was at the height of its power and territorial extent. Tenochtitlan, the Aztec capital, was one of the largest cities in the world, with a population estimated at 200,000–400,000. The Aztec Empire controlled a vast tributary network stretching from the Gulf of Mexico to the Pacific Ocean. However, the empire was already facing internal tensions: tributary states resented Aztec tribute demands, and there were religious and political divisions within the Aztec elite. These internal tensions would later be exploited by Hernán Cortés and his Spanish conquistadors. In 1517, however, the Aztec Empire appeared to be stable and powerful, unaware of the Spanish threat gathering on its coasts.

sources.name: Florentine Codex (Sahagún's compilation of Nahua accounts)
Archaeological evidence from Mexico City excavations

events.certainty_note: The characterization of the Aztec Empire as being 'at its height' in 1517 is based on archaeological and indigenous sources. The population estimates for Tenochtitlan are debated, with modern scholarship suggesting figures ranging from 200,000 to 400,000.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Continues Inward Focus; Zhengde Emperor Rules
events.description: In 1517, the Ming Dynasty, under the Zhengde Emperor (who had ruled since 1505), continued its policy of inward focus and continental defense. The Zhengde Emperor was known for his unconventional behavior and his reliance on eunuchs, which created tension with the Confucian scholar-official establishment. By 1517, the Ming Dynasty had definitively abandoned the great oceanic voyages of Zheng He (which had ended in 1433) and had focused instead on land-based defense against Mongol incursions and internal consolidation. The Ming Dynasty would remain the dominant power in East Asia for the rest of the 16th century.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broader pattern of Ming inward focus is well-documented, though the specific year 1517 is somewhat arbitrary. The political dynamics were a gradual process spanning decades.

events.title: Spanish Crown Consolidates Power in Castile and Aragon; Charles I Inherits Spanish Throne
events.description: In 1517, Charles I (later Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor) inherited the Spanish throne following the death of his maternal grandfather, Ferdinand of Aragon. Charles was the son of Joanna the Mad (daughter of Isabella and Ferdinand) and Philip the Handsome (son of Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor). Charles's inheritance of the Spanish throne, combined with his later election as Holy Roman Emperor (1519), made him the most powerful ruler in Europe. In 1517, Charles was still in the Netherlands and had not yet traveled to Spain to consolidate his rule. His arrival in Spain in 1518 would be met with some resistance from Spanish nobles and cities, but he would eventually establish his authority.
key_figures: Charles I (later Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor)
Ferdinand of Aragon (Charles's grandfather, previous Spanish king)
events.certainty_note: Charles I's inheritance of the Spanish throne in 1517 is well-documented in Spanish royal records. The date is universally accepted.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative that Luther's posting of the 95 Theses on 31 October 1517 was the singular 'beginning' of the Reformation is somewhat misleading. Modern scholarship emphasizes that the Reformation was a complex, gradual process with multiple causes and participants, and that Luther's theses were not immediately recognized as revolutionary. The theses were written in Latin and were intended for academic debate; their rapid spread and translation into German was somewhat unexpected. Additionally, the characterization of the Ottoman conquest of Egypt as a decisive turning point is sometimes overstated; while the conquest was militarily significant, the Ottoman Empire had already been the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean before 1517. The narrative of Spanish consolidation under Charles I is also somewhat misleading, as Charles faced significant resistance from Spanish nobles and cities, and his authority was not fully established until the 1520s.
historiographic_note: The year 1517 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding Luther's 95 Theses and the Ottoman conquest of Egypt. The Reformation is one of the most thoroughly studied periods of European history, with extensive primary and secondary sources. However, documentation is sparse for many regions, particularly Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands. The year is famous in Western historiography for Luther's theses, but this focus reflects a Western-centric bias; the Ottoman conquest of Egypt was equally significant in global history, but receives less attention in Western sources. The documentation is heavily weighted toward Europe and the Atlantic world; Asian and African events are less thoroughly recorded in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Luther's 95 Theses of 1517 led to his summons to Rome and wider attention in 1518.
The Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517 established Ottoman dominance that Suleiman I would consolidate in 1520.
Charles I's inheritance of the Spanish throne in 1517 set the stage for his election as Holy Roman Emperor in 1519.
